comments.title: Dolmen Arch and Indian philosophy
comments.content:
I'm curious about the provenance of some of the Dolmen Arch teachings, specifically:

(1) the three principles of substance, motion, and consciousness (calas, gwyar, and nwyfre), and

(2) the duality between Annwn and Awen, with Annwn as the realm of manifestation (toggling between unmanifest latency, and the fully manifest cosmos) and Awen as superimposed and all-pervasive, yet in itself unchanging and impassive.

I ask because while studying the Dolmen Arch course, I've also been reviewing some of the oldest strata of Indian metaphysics, in preparation for an online course I'll be teaching this January and February. To a first approximation, in the Sāṃkhya tradition of Hindu philosophy, we spell the above-mentioned teachings as the three guṇas of tamas, rajas, and sattva, and the duality between prakṛti and puruṣa.

To be sure, a lot more complexities enter in, in the second approximation! In addition to the uncanny similarities, there are also critical and profound differences in terms of explanation (how the cosmos is), evaluation (whether that situation is good, bad, or indifferent), and practical response (what we should do when confronted by it).

As a result, I've had many weeks worth of incredibly fruitful meditations, exploring these parallels and divergences! And I've come away from those meditations with a much deeper appreciation of both traditions.

Still, I'm curious whether the parallels are a matter of simple convergence, or of ancestry. Can you help to shed any light on the history of the Dolmen Arch material, in this regard?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch and Indian philosophy
replies.content: Calas, gwyar, and nwyfre can be found in Iolo Morganwg's Barddas, which collects papers he wrote in the late 18th and early 19th centuries. My Welsh isn't good enough to know whether they appear in this sense in earlier Welsh literature. The concepts of Awen and Annwn are found in medieval Welsh literature, but I know of nothing older than the Dolmen Arch course that pairs them in that way. It's quite possible that the Dolmen Arch's presentation of the three principles and of Awen and Annwn was shaped by the corresponding comments from Hindu metaphysics, since by the time the course was written (in the 1920s, from internal evidence) English translations of Hindu philosophical texts had been in circulation in the American occult scene for more than forty years.

replies.title: Re: The Red God
replies.content:
Synchronicity strikes again! Looking up Ernest Thompson Seton's works on archive.org and hathitrust.org, I happened on esoteric writings by two women named Grace Gallatin Seton and Julia Moss Seton. And then wikipedia informede me that these women were Ernest Thompson Seton's first and second wife, and that each of them--unsurprisingly!--was involved with his Woodcraft movement.

However, each of them was also a published occultist, and this suggests to me that early Woodcraft may have been more inspired by occult teachings than Ernest Seton himself let on in his handbooks for the movement.

Grace Gallatin Thompson contributed to a volume by a (now forgotten) independent esotericist, Carl Henrik Andreas Bjerregaard, titled The Great Mother: A Gospel of the Eternally Feminine; Occult and Scientific Studies in the Sacred and Secret Life (1913). Shades of Sjöö and Mor's The Great Cosmic Mother (1987)! Wikipedia mentions that she was a Theosophist.

Bjerregaard wa a fascinating and deep-minded occultist, who had contacts in the H. B. of L. (Hermetic Brotherthood of Light/Luxor) and its offshoots as well as in several branches of Theosophy, as well as being the head of his own small Theosophical Lodge in New York City. He was Librarian of the Astor Library, now incorporated into the New York Public Library, which gave him unparalleled access to its occult treasures. He published quite a number of esoteric works, pretty much all of which can now be downloaded here and there.

Julia Moss Seton, in contrast, was involved in the New Thought Movement, and wrote several books and pamphlets presenting aspects of its teachings. One of them was titled The Psychology of the Solar Plexus and Subconscious Mind (1914). IIRC, we touched on teachings about the solar plexus as a second brain a while ago; this pamphlet of hers may shed some light on the antecedents of that idea.

So there may be depths as yet unplumbed in the forgotten (pre)history of Wicca that our host's articles have brought to light.

replies.title: Re: AZ Horoscope Maker edition
replies.content:
Perhaps only in the US, and that for editions published before 1925 (before 1926 starting 2020, Jan 1st); George Llewellyn died in 1954, which in principle means 2025 for 70 years of death. As far as I know, US uses publication date for copyright. For example, all George Llewellyn books published in the UK will be out of copyright in 2025, but in the US, only those published before 1930.

In short, copyright varies for each country. It is always necessary to verify the laws in your particular country. I think (don't quote me on this; consult a lawyer) that books in public domain in the US actually are so only there, depending on the date of the author's death, because of the Berne Convention.

replies.title: Re: Blessings
replies.content:
It was a very very old tradition in the British Isles that the ploughman, when he opens up the first furrow at the start of the planting season each year, should bury a bit of food from his own lunch at the start of the first opening that he makes in the earth.

A much more elaborate version of the same ritual in Old English, involving two days of ritual action, was written down sometime in the first half of the 11th century. You can find the full text (with a modern English translation) on pp. 172-177 of Felix Grendon's The Anglo-Saxon Charms (1909), available online at archive.org here:

https://archive.org/details/jstor-534353/page/n73/mode/1up

I published a short analysis of the Old English ritual in an article titled "Frazer's Two Laws of Magic (Notes Toward a General Theory of Magic, Part II)" in The Witches' Almanac for 2018-2019, which is issue #37 of that popular periodical.

comments.title: Ogham alphabet
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

firstly, I'd like to thank you for all the work you've done for us this year. It is highly appreciated.

Secondly, the question - it has recently come to my attention that the order of the Ogham fews in the first aicme is subject of some controversy. Mainstream researchers say its BLFSN and that competing theories that it was originally BLNFS are disproven (so says Wikipedia). The DMH gives the BLNFS order, because of Belinus, the sun god.

Its not a big deal for me, I personally like the DMH version. But do you know how letter order in an alphabet became a controversy among researchers? It's just such a strange topic to disagree on, there got to be direct evidence one way or another.

And it can create some problems down the line if somebody decides to use Ogham letters to encode numbers or the other way around.

replies.title: Re: Ogham alphabet
replies.content: If the book says BLNFS, then BLNFS it is? Independent of the historicity, what's in the book must be used. The system was probably designed to suit that order. Or so I think, of course. John?

comments.title: Geomancy and the Dolmen Arch
comments.content:
I'd always been interested in the "theurgic" method of Geomancy as recounted in your Art and Practice of Geomancy: prayer accompanied by tracing out a renaissance symbol for the relevant planetary intelligence. But putting it into application, it never quite felt like it was right for me somehow. I guess it's that outside of geomancy, I was spending no time forming a relationship with these entities, nor did I feel any particular feeling of invitation to do so.

I'd been experimenting for a couple of weeks with using the the same general method, but making a blanket substitution with the deities from The Dolmen Arch associated with each planet, and the symbols as given in the first grade. It seemed to work okay. Then I discovered last week that I'd been perhaps doing it "wrong", in that the symbols in magical application for the deities relevant to Jupiter and Saturn were simple clockwise circles of a certain color, with (maybe?) no need for a banishing untracing, and no symbol at all for the Moon. Hmmm.

Last night I did a Geomancy reading: "What effects does it have on my readings to do the ritual I've been doing lately using Dolmen Arch symbols, praying to the deity who symbolically corresponds to the question?" (I hadn't actually asked this yet, choosing instead to simply try the method for a bit first.) The court came out as Right Witness Fortuna Major, Left Witness Populus, Judge Fortuna Major. So clearly I'm on the right track here, I just need to settle on the exact drawing method for the deities of Jupiter, Saturn, and Moon.

(1) Based on your own knowledge, would you say it's probably better to draw out these symbols in the form in which they first got introduced with circles and crosses, or in the "magical" form with only circles (or nothing at all for Moon)?

(2a) If circles and crosses, do you recommend a particular stroke order for the crosses? Or should I just be consistent, and generally clockwise?
(2b) If just circles, I guess it's no drawn symbol for the Moon, and as I pray just visualize myself as I would at the same point in the SOP?

(3) As with the SOP, is it unnecessary to banish at the end if the symbols in question are the ones related to Jupiter, Saturn, and the Moon?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Dolmen Arch
replies.content:
1) Draw them out in their full form.

2) Consistent and clockwise is good, and use the appropriate symbol for the Moon.

3) That's correct.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Dolmen Arch
replies.content: Thank you, this is extremely helpful to me.

comments.title: A Conscious Group Within Society
comments.content:
Good Morning, Mr. Greer,

I hope you don’t mind my asking you a question on consciousness and civilization.

I know you’re skeptical there will be a sudden jump in humanity’s consciousness, leading to the salvation of civilization. However I wonder, if just a small number of people — a fraction of the population — were to work hard to increase their own consciousness, could they act as an outsized influence on humanity as a whole?

There is a theory I’ve heard, that if there were only a few hundred truly conscious individuals in the world, they would be able to influence all of humanity (a Gurdjieffian theory). Do you think a percentage of conscious people could help the world preserve its cultural, spiritual, and scientific treasures?

Generally I agree with your predictions in The Long Decline and your other books. However l’m wondering if there is some hope for civilization, if enough people can generate enough consciousness.

Do you think a group of conscious people within humanity can influence the whole of mankind, and thereby help to preserve our cultural gems? Or is it more likely that a small number of conscious individuals will wait out the coming dark age, and be the creators of a future civilization?

As always, Thank You for everything!

replies.title: Re: A Conscious Group Within Society
replies.content:
Ah, the old Hundredth Monkey theory. Ask yourself this: if that were true, wouldn't it also be the case that if a small number of people were to work hard at decreasing their consciousness, that would also have an outsized effect on humanity as a whole, in the other direction? That's not just a thought experiment, after all. The recent opiate epidemic is one good test case; the rise of fascism in the 1930s is another...

No one's going to "wait out" the coming dark age, as four or five centuries is longer than any individual or group of individuals can wait, you know! The rise and fall of civilizations is an organic process; they have their life cycles and their fallow periods. What can help, right here and now, is for people who care about the legacies of our current civilization to choose parts of our heritage that matter to them, and make a sustained effort to get those into as many hands as possible, so that those things have an increased chance of making it through the dark ages and reaching the waiting hands of the future. There's much work to be done along those lines.

replies.title: Re: A Conscious Group Within Society
replies.content: You are living inside the jump in humanity’s consciousness, fueled by crude oil. After population peaks, there will never be so much humans in this planet. I'm afraid I'm not very impressed with its quality.

replies.title: Re: A Conscious Group Within Society
replies.content: Well, yes. It's a central part of the myth of modernity that we're always on the brink of some mighty change, and certain very special people are going to catalyze that change; I don't think any of the people who wallowed in that narrative ever thought that the "special people" would be quite so clueless, or that the mighty change in question would be a bigger than usual version of decline and fall...

replies.title: Re: A Conscious Group Within Society
replies.content:
Please consider this from a traditional perspective.

There are already groups of hundreds of people that did work very hard and that did (and continue to) influence the state of consciousness of Humanity as a whole. Christians call these people Saints, but they are present in every culture and religious tradition. I will just speak of Saints because that's what I am familiar with.

You will notice, when you read the hagiographies, that saints do not start out gathering a bunch of people, deciding they are going to work really hard, and then everyone turns into a saint within a decade or two. Most often than not, they live their lives within communities full of normal people that are not quite as devoted to achieve Sainthood. Sometimes saints influence those people closer to them and help them achieve way more than they would have if left to their own devices, but it is quite rare (though not unheard of) for one senior saint to mentor one or two junior saints through one lifetime. A full litter of them? virtually impossible.

More over, if you see what those people did, it is no wonder there are so few takers for the sainthood position. Saints come in many shapes and forms, but the one thing they have in common is that the thing they choose as the work of their life ends up consuming all of their time and all of their lives. You cannot get sainthood lessons Mondays and Thursdays from 5pm to 7pm. I know I don't want to do that, and I know that I could not do that even if I deceived myself into thinking that I want to.

comments.title: Geomancy and the Tetractys
comments.content:
Here’s one I’ve been sitting on a while, but the fact that this is the last MM for a month has prompted me to pull it out of hiding.

It's been a while since I've seen it asked here, but at least a couple of times, people have asked here or on the other blog about whether there is a connection between the Tetractys and Geomancy. If I'm recalling correctly, your reply both times was that it was a good topic for meditation.

(1) I would like to ask whether you are aware that Geomantic Shield charts are not merely similar to Tetractyses, but in fact, exactly and precisely perfect Tetractyses (with “unnecessary” bits edited out in their normal presentation)? And whether it's something you recall seeing discussed anywhere? I haven't been able to find any discussion of this anywhere else except for, giving credit where it’s due, a time where Sam Block once got 95% of the way there on his blog. (Though I won’t be linking his blog here, as per his desires. Interested parties can find it easily enough with a web search.)

If this is all something that you wished to leave for students to discover in meditation, I won’t clarify what I mean. But on the off chance you’re not quite getting what I’m talking about, may I have permission to post a fuller explanation here? I can explain it fairly simply and quickly, but it will take more space than a regular Magic Monday question, and I don’t want to impose.

(2) I haven’t ever formally studied Cabala, but I recall that you have said that Cabala was derived from the Tetractys— is that right? If the Tree of Life is a Tetractys, and every Geomancy chart is a Tetractys, then it seems reasonable that it would be very fruitful to meditate on direct correspondences between the Tree of Life and the houses of Geomantic divination. (Although in my own case I should probably wait until such time as I actually study Cabala a bit first.)

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Tetractys
replies.content:
1) Well, it's not exactly a tetractys, because the tetractys increases by one step each level --

[IMAGE:0]

-- while the geomantic chart doubles (or halves) with each level. If you've got some way of getting around that mathematical difference, I'll be interested in hearing it.

2) Yes, you should certainly study some Cabala first, but given that, by all means meditate on the subject!

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Tetractys
replies.content:
It’s a tetractys inverted from the form that you give it—4321 as opposed to 1234, but yes, it’s a tetractys.

Begin with a normal set of four mothers. Instead of just adding Mother1+Mother2 and M3+M4, add M2+3 as well, and the same for the nieces, Tetractys-style. This produces an extra niece in between Nieces 1 and 2, which we can call Niece "1.5", as well as another line below the Nieces, which I'll designate as "Cousins" to go with the Mothers and Nieces.


M4      M3      M2      M1
   N2      N1.5     N1
        C2      C1
            RW


What's fascinating is that numerically, the Right Witness achieved through the tetractys method is always exactly the same as the RW which results from the traditional method, no matter what four Mothers you start with. Meanwhile, on the other side with the Daughters of the Mothers: 


D4      D3      D2      D1
   N4      N3.5     N3
        C4      C3
            LW



Once again, the Left Witness is exactly the same as what would have resulted with a traditional calculation.

In other words, the triads of three “extra characters” on each side (a trio of one “half niece” and two cousins to right and to left), C1, C2 on the right, and N3.5, C3, and C4 on the left) lead to the same court and the same Judge whether they are there or not.

Go ahead and try this layout with any real life geomantic chart, and you’ll see— it works.

Of course, there’s the issue of there this being *two* tetractyses rather than one. But I would argue that in the same way that the points of the Mothers are exactly the same as the points of the Daughters seen from a different direction, giving a three dimensional view of two images of the same set of facts, the same thing is going on here: the two tetractyses are in fact one with their points rearranged. However this is getting rather long as it is so let’s move on for now.

It seems very clear, to me at least, that Geomancy began as a single “3D Tetractys” with the Judge resolving both 2D tetracti into a single balanced view of the whole situation. But probably at a fairly early point, the characters which could be skipped over for calculation of the court got edited out of the process as a mathematical trick.

This explains why the Shield Triplicities are arranged the way they are, as well as Company of Houses (Company in 9-10 and 11-12 being higher order Triplicities than the traditional first four). The extraneous characters are like structurally unnecessary ornaments on the front of the Shield; haunted but apparently uninhabited houses in between Houses in the neighborhood.

(Also, it may shed new light on why Shield charts became fancifully illustrated as literal shields: a traditional Archbishop's coat of arms is two perfectly balanced tetractyses sitting on either side of a shield, with the Judge, er, Archbishop, in the middle-- exactly like Shield Charts, in this this tetractysian view of them.)

Regarding the paired triads of extra characters, it seemed to me that they might still contain divinatory meanings of their own, if only secondary. I consulted Geomancy about this prospect and my interpretation of the response was that there are indeed such meanings to be found, but that I am not myself equipped to decipher them at the current time.

To anyone whom this may be new information, I freely share it in case anyone may be interested in exploring these mysterious characters further. Also, I’ll be creating a post about it shortly over at the little Geomancy discussion group Conjunctio shortly, should anyone wish to discuss this all further at a more leisurely pace than Magic Monday allows.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Tetractys
replies.content: Um, so it's a tetractys if you add a bunch of dots that aren't there in the original pattern. Got it. Well, I suppose so; if that works for you, by all means.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Tetractys
replies.content: The extra dots aren’t just inserted by me on some sort of personal whim. They are the logical extension of adding together a more mathematically obvious pattern M1+M2, M2+M3, M3+M4. Why would the first entity to ever devise a geomantic chart skip straight over M3+M4? For anyone who has ever wondered why M2+M3 gets skipped over when calculating a chart, or why the triplicities are chosen as they are, or why the patterns of company of houses are arranged as they are, this explanation provides a simple answer: Geomancy is divination by tetractys. Due to the mathematical properties of the addition of oddand even points in a tetractys, though, it is not necessary to write out the full tetractys in order to achieve the court.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Tetractys
replies.content: The pattern of adding by pairs doesn't seem especially non-obvious to me! Still, as I noted, if it works for you, by all means.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and the Tetractys
replies.content:
That’s fair enough. Well, at the very least, without any speculation as to the origins of geomancy, one can definitively say that it’s possible to add singly instead of in pairs, and still achieve every character in a traditional geomantic chart; and that it’s possible to fit any traditional geomantic chart within the larger structure of a tetactrys, still owning all of the same odd-even addition rules at every point.

Certainly, to each their own delights; but as for me, I think that’s pretty fascinating!

comments.title: Blessing magic for Europe
comments.content:
I have been performing a blessing candle this year for my loved ones in high-exposure jobs (prison guards etc).

I was planning to ask whether blessing candle magic could become something viably potent if I put decades into it (I'm also working through DMH & DA). My reasoning being that if ever things got super bad when I was old, I could cast some huge blessing and fire all of my guns at once and explode into space.

Then I read that any country that has Mars in the tenth house will be at war within twenty years, which includes a large swath of Europe (could someone list the specific countries?).

So my question is: could European Ecosophians start blessing magic now, build it for several years, then if something pops off in 2038, say, that magic could actually accomplish something?

We would ground that magic in the material plane by taking measures in our own communities along a certain theme. We could choose a word, a positive one, so we're not contemplating war: say it's Stability. This could involve Green Wizardry things, learning a skill or supporting craftsmen (in my case it's economic relocalization: I've moved my shopping to small local businesses). Thus Ecosophians could work toward Stability even if we don't agree on political issues, like if two British people are on opposite sides of the Brexit debate, etc.

This would keep the egregores separate because it would span many traditions and be an individual effort (dare I say, a standalone complex). That weakens it compared to what Dion Fortune did in The Magical Battle for Britain, but things are so astrally toxic I think it might be necessary. If Betsy in Belgium goes septic and starts worshiping demons, we don't want it to taint the whole sphere.

It would be "come as you are": if you've got a flat full of kids and all you can do is plug in an electric candle and pray every few days, fine. Do it. Those of us with more time and space in our lives will carry more weight.

It wouldn't even need central communication, in case we lose access to the internet. If something happens to where you wonder whether the other Ecosophians in Europe have switched their blessing magic from daily concerns to praying for Europe, you're probably right! Thank you for your time.

replies.title: Re: Blessing magic for Europe
replies.content: Candle magic is quite a robust technique, which is why it's so popular. For those who don't have the training to do serious ceremonial magic, it's one of the best options. So, yes, you can do that, but be very careful and do plenty of divination before you settle on a theme. One of the most common causes of Stability in history, for example, is a really big war -- afterward, things tend to be stable for a long time!

comments.title: Neopagans, Wiccans and messy lives: a theory
comments.content:
It's come up a few times over the years in JMG's blogs that there seem to be a disproportionate number of neopagans, and especially Wiccans, who have a lot of problems in their lives.

My theory (which makes a number of assumptions. It would need some sociology research to test!)
Firstly, to be attracted to paganism in the first place, people are usually dissatisfied with Christianity, and they feel a spiritual impulse, which means they aren't content to be atheists, agnostics or 'nones', as a lot of other modern people do when they leave the faith they were brought up in, if they even grew up in one.

I reckon that most of these people had problems in life which predate their involvement in neopaganism, for example they experienced childhood abuse or other traumatic disruption, and/or they are chronically ill etc. (Abuse predisposes to health problems in later life too.) Having problems like these, and finding that there isn't enough support in the world, can make it more likely a person searches for supernatural explanations and especially solutions.

The other 'alternative' religion a dissatisfied Westerner with hippie tendencies is most likely to turn to is Buddhism. However, depending what type of buddhism the seeker encounters, it might seem overly stringent and unsympathetic, when the person is looking for comfort and support. Buddhism can be about denial of the world, about someone getting the impression they are supposed to be in pain. Depending what type of Christianity the person left, this might sound altogether too much like their family's religion. Austerity and endurance might look like important parts of Buddhism, and this type of person is already less able to deal with an extra helping of those, on top of their daily life, than the average person. The younger ones (I don't know if this also applied in the 1960s) may have already heard criticisms of the doctrine of karma as demeaning to disabled people, another reason to write it off.

Having a lot of problems to deal with in life may mean that the person has less time for types of philosophy that seem far away from practical experience, like it's said that women were traditionally less interested in pondering the nature of reality because they had to feed the kids and do the chores, and those were real enough. The problems of reality may intrude too much for Buddhism to connect with this type of seeker, who may be dealing with illness, practical problems, difficult people and other drama.

All this is of course happening in a society where rationalist materialism is so pervasive most of us barely think about it, like fish in water. It seems normal and right to think that this world and experience in it matters a lot, very different from serious Buddhism or some types of Christianity.

Pagan religions can seem to be more about getting needs met and getting stuff, considering accounts of transactional worship and interactions with gods in mythology. They often celebrate life in this world as a matter of course, and certainly more than the harder doctrines of other major religions do.

The increase in left-wing wicca and neopaganism during the Trump presidency was another way of trying to get needs met and get some control back, because rationalist avenues didn't seem to be doing enough, and they felt the administration was so uniquely dreadful (as had actually been said about the Bushes and Reagan before) that they needed something more. The modern left has a very external locus of control and with magic and witchcraft they are trying to harness that and gain some sense of control back. Whilst they talk of self-care, and there are passing mentions of therapy, it's conservatives who currently emphasise working on yourself and the internal locus of control, like Jordan Peterson, "do you even lift bro", and young men who call themselves Stoics.

For women who have difficult lives and who trace back many of their problems to men, a grouping of alternative women and a mother goddess makes sense, with its rejection of patriarchy in both its mystical forms as Abrahamic religion, of the historic witch-hunts, and modern rationalist forms such as those who expect science to explain everything.

I don't think this explains why Neopaganism and Wicca don't help more people improve their lives (aside from a lot of people with problems gathering and generating the drama that was already normal to them) but may explain why these people are drawn to it.

From what I've seen of books on Wicca, there is a lot of emphasis on rituals and magic, and romantic stuff about being a witch, and not nearly as much on working on yourself, compared to what you put on this blog. Some posters here report improvements from regular practices like will training, meditation and journaling, which resemble different forms of therapy but aren't as expensive. I suspect that as well as the idea of the witch as negative and marginal, the lack of emphasis on practices like these (among people who could really do with it, and often actual therapy) has something to do with it.

This might be a 'take it to your own blog' sort of post, but as it responds to something that comes up in this community from time to time I thought it would be of interest to some here.

replies.title: Re: Neopagans, Wiccans and messy lives: a theory
replies.content:
Thanks for your thoughtful work in parsing out a possible explanation for the troubles others experience and why their religious leanings might lead to some amelioration of distress, yet not address underlying issues.

As you say, people whose early life is marred by childhood experiences and-or historical social bias in favor of men, money, or racially motivated malice might very well be seeking the nurturing of a mother-goddess and validation of their own feelings of injustice. Cultural cults’ blatant disrespect and marginalization of certain class interests can be psychically damaging.

Without heroine tales to match hero tales, how would a girl recognize herself as someone who is brave? Cinderella only tells her to have small feet and pretty dresses to succeed. When all deities are depicted as white, it would be a rare child whose spiritual growth is informed by inner visions of a black Mary or Jesus, although these certainly exist.

On the other hand, people who endure prolonged episodes of helplessness as children may not be able (without guidance) to progress beyond the stage of needing psychic nurture——their babyhood in the spirit. Their stature, mind, and emotional ‘muscles’ do not grow unless they are exercised.

Perhaps, unknown to me, secret Wiccan esoteric principles may offer such strengthening exercises, but few people commit to a path long enough to attain character growth.

So, there ought to be an exoteric component to a religion that fosters sustained effort so that such ‘lay people’ have a means to improve themselves even if they do not ‘join up’ fully. Maybe Wicca has fallen short in this area. External measures can serve as a touchstone for proof of internal attainment.

Charity to the poor, for instance: Calvinism in Christianity slid all too easily from the stance of self-improvement to the stance of despising the poor as unworthy. Likewise, the unwise use of cursing in Wicca. People who have been long powerless are seldom skilled at handing the reins of power.

Calvinists who are hard and grasping and Wiccans who fling curses about freely are not practicing religion but self-indulgence. As the Bible says: by their fruits, ye shall know them.

I appreciate your conciliatory attitude and willingness to suspend harsh critical judgment of facts that may look like personal failure, but could instead be a partial success. Falling short of an ideal is not uncommon for human beings.

As a mass of leaves sustains a tree, though each is imperfect in form and genetic expression, so can a mass of people sustain a Tree of Life in our material midst. Even those whose expression of the Goddess is weak may be contributing to the future. By helping to revivify the feminine in religion, a great work may have been begun, even if it is far from finished.

replies.title: Re: Neopagans, Wiccans and messy lives: a theory
replies.content: I think this explains some of it, but I think you're missing a crucial point -- forms of inner work that are badly designed and badly carry out really can mess you over. I'm fairly sure that some of the pop-Wicca methods in circulation -- not the old-fashioned initiatory Wicca, which is fairly well designed, but the stuff that Starhawk, Scott Cunningham, et al. helped launch into circulation -- has seriously unbalanced effects when used by the inexperienced and clueless, and I suspect that played a large role in the rise of Neopagan disability culture et al.

comments.content:
A Jovial (Jovian?) Yuletide Tale

More an amusing anecdote, but: Last week, I had made my customary tribute to Zeus (a portion of beef offered up under the open sky) along with the usual invocation, but, unusually for me, I also petitioned for some hospitable weather on Christmas Eve. We were having our regular celebration outside because of the pandemic, and severe storms were forecast for the area. I was hoping things would go forward for the sake of my mom, who is Christian and had been very desirous of seeing the family on the holiday.

As it turns out, the storms held until late evening and a good time was had. While most of my family was congregating in the front yard, waiting for the annual arrival of Santa aboard a fire truck, I felt compelled to offer thanks to the Aegis-bearer for the reprieve. As there was no beef around, and against my better judgement, I used a portion of pork for the offering, casting it into the fire we had going.

Two things then happened. The Santa surrogate and the fire apparatus rolled onto our street, sirens blazing. At the same time, a giant gust of wind lifted up the tent we had and sent it into the fire pit, knocking said pit over and sending embers everywhere.

In my mind, a peal of raucous laughter--definitely not "ho ho ho."

Fortunately, no fires were set to any structures and no one was injured.

Roberto Calasso said, paraphrasing: "there is nothing sadder than a prayer to the wrong god." I would add, there is nothing scarier than the wrong offering to the right god.

My takeaway: Lesson learned and message received--we will stick with the usual offering, no substitutes. I realize I will need to do some work on that front, and it's a mess of my own making.

But, per your Santa post yesterday, I have to wonder (and here's the question): Given the concurrence of the appearance of the Santa surrogate, is there some sort of correlation with the entity known as Santa? Is he working on behalf of some other henad, or...?

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Everyone experiences doubt from time to time about their most deeply held convictions and beliefs. We sometimes feel that the path we invested so much time and energy on is a dead end. Even fanaticism can be conceived as an overcompensation for a crippling feeling of insecurity. In my own life, I’ve completely revamped my worldview several times in response to new information. However, I’ve never been in the position of making an income from promoting a certain philosophy or world view, which has allowed me to transition relatively seamlessly from one to another, without having to justify myself to an audience and facing a potential loss of income. As the famous Upton Sinclair quote goes: ““It is difficult to get a man to understand something, when his salary depends on his not understanding it.”

In this respect, have you ever experienced any doubt about your own convictions, regarding the occult world view, and the social theories you’ve promoted about the collapse of industrial civilization or the myth of progress? Have you ever felt out of your element or unqualified to make the sweeping conclusions that you’ve made over the years, and if you were ever to feel in such a way, would you consider recanting?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Questions like this are a reliable source of amusement for me, because they assume as a matter of course that the occult worldview, say, can't possibly be grounded in practical experience. I've practiced magic since 1975; at this point I have a very good idea, based on a vast amount of direct personal experience, of what it can and cannot do; I use it constantly and get reliable results from it. You might as well walk up to someone on the street and say in an earnest tone, "Excuse me, ma'am, have you ever considered the possibility that you don't have a nose?" The person you're addressing knows perfectly well that she has a nose, because she dabbed makeup on it earlier that day!

In the same way, my discussions in print and elsewhere of the decline (ahem; not, please notice, "collapse") of industrial society are based on the fact that I've been watching it happen for decades. Do you remember when the mainstream was awash with confident claims that by now we'd have cities on the Moon, human bootprints on Mars, cheap abundant electricity from fusion reactors, cures for cancer and the common cold, et cetera, ad infinitum, ad nauseam? I do. I also know that anyone back in 1970, say, who predicted that in 2020 we would be where in fact we are this year, in terms of technological progress and its consequences, would have been shouted down as a hopeless pessimist awash in misanthropic gloom. And yet here we are, well down the initial slope of decline, while true believers in progress still insist that we're on our way to the stars.

I reached my conclusions about the nature of reality and the future of industrial civilization gradually, over the course of many years of study and observation and, in the case of magic, daily practice. I have continued to develop and refine them over the years. I'm quite aware of my own limitations as a student of occultism on the one hand, and a student of history on the other, but I'm also aware that my take on things quite consistently results in accurate predictions, while those who embrace blind faith in materialism and progress, and draw sweeping conclusions on the basis of those beliefs, just as consistently make predictions that bellyflop. That being the case, we might want to talk about exactly who should consider recanting their beliefs...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
This is a question about paradigms, in the vein of Thomas Kuhn’s The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. For those who aren’t familiar, according to Kuhn, human beings always live in a paradigm, which is like an all-encompassing world view or theory of the world that attempts to explain the totality of lived experience. When it comes to paradigms, it seems that there is always an opposition between what I’ll call the “center” and the “fringe.” The center represents established thought, the conventional opinion and theories about the world that dominate a certain era and give its ‘flavor.’ The fringe represents the unorthodox thinkers, those who are sufficiently detached from the center to offer alternatives to the dominant theories. The fringe abounds with heretics and conspiracy theorists.

As I see it, the center is mostly right in offering satisfactory explanations about the world, until they’re proven catastrophically wrong and the paradigm is overthrown. However, the latter event occurs only very infrequently, in epochal transitions from one paradigm to another. In normal times, the center is able to integrate or explain away anomalies and thus maintain the paradigm. For most people, most of the time, the center represents the safe option, and the guardians of the paradigm usually do a pretty good job of filtering what doesn’t belong and making sure people stay on the straight and narrow path.
By contrast, the fringe is mostly wrong until they’re proven catastrophically right, and they replace the center. However, at any given time, the fringe is filled with a wild cacophony of different theories with no mainstream credibility (by definition, otherwise they wouldn’t be on the fringe). Most of those theories will die before reaching the status of an accepted paradigm, and the thinkers promoting them will fade away into obscurity without ever reaching the iconoclastic status of a Galileo.

In our own age, the scientific establishment obviously represents the center, the safe, accepted opinion with mainstream credibility and institutional support, just as Roman Catholicism filled the same role in the Middle Ages. Meanwhile, the occult philosophy is on the fringe, along with many other unorthodox systems of belief. I would obviously consider JMG himself to be a figure of the fringe, in this model, given that he has no accepted role in the center: no academic position, no institutional support, no publications in mainstream scientific journals (the litmus test of modern epistemic authority), and so on.

Given the above, isn’t the safest option for most people, at any given time, to go along with the center and ignore the fringe? What I’m proposing here is a kind of epistemic Pascal’s Wager: We cannot help but live in a paradigm, and the center provides numerous social and economic advantages. In addition, it has the benefit of stability, while the fringe is insecure and uncertain. Moreover, human beings are not omniscient, and the only certainty in history seems to be change itself. The certainties of one age are the laughing stock of another. Given that absolute knowledge is out of reach, the sensible thing for the majority is to go with what’s convenient.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Too funny. I am indeed a figure of the fringe, and in fact have discussed that fact here and on my blog at some length. What your analysis neglects is that the consensus model of reality produced by every society is always flawed; there are always plenty of facts, as Kuhn points out, that it does not make room for, and some of those are inevitably of great importance to at least some people. Furthermore, the consensus model of reality inevitably turns into a support for the social status quo, and thus a way of limiting access to the social and economic advantages you mention to those who are already "on the inside" for one reason or another, be it social class or success in climbing the ranks of a hierarchical institution such as the Catholic Church or the Communist party in a socialist nation.

That's where the fringe becomes a lively and necessary part of the equation. On the fringe, it's possible to discuss the inconvenient facts that are excluded by the consensus model; on the fringe, it's possible for people of talent who don't happen to have whatever factors make for success within the establishment to establish self-supporting scenes of their own. Nor is the consensus model as stable or permanent as you've suggested; au contraire, it's always shifting and flowing, so that what is acceptable in one decade may be flung out to the fringes in the next, or vice versa. Thus the fringe is where new ideas are born and tested, and where old ideas that still have value can be salvaged. It's an exciting, challenging place to be, the fringes; it's where the action is.

Of course if you're more comfortable sitting in the cozy center, with every threatening reality excluded from your view by blind faith in the current paradigm, hey, by all means enjoy your life. Some of us prefer a different option.

comments.title: More American History
comments.content:
A datapoint for your alternative American history -
https://publicdomainreview.org/essay/lord-of-misrule-thomas-mortons-american-subversions

Thomas Morton attempted to found an explicitly "pagan" colony in Massachusetts in 1626. He was rounded up and sent back to England by Myles Standish only a couple of years later. Standish was not impressed by the Maypole, nor by the fact that Morton encouraged intermarriage between the colonists and the native Algonquins.

That Public Domain website actually has a lot of really interesting things in it!

Squirrelly Jen

replies.title: Re: More American History
replies.content:
Actually, this account somewhat whitewashes Myles Standish and Plymouth Colony. They didn't send Morton back to England in fetters, but marooned him on the (mostly barren) Isles of Shoals to starve to death. His Native American friends brought him food and kept him alive until a boat from England happened to arrive on which he could return to England. The Colonists probably thought about killing Morton outright, but if so, they were deterred by the extent of his connections with the rich and powerful in England.

Morton came back to New England twice, in each case with the help of an ancestor of mine, Isaac Allerton. He allied himself with the Royalist, Church-of-England--very much anti-Puritan!--colonial ventures of Sir Fernando Gorges in what is now Maine. He died and was buried somewhere in Agomenticus (now York), but the whereabouts of his grave have been lost for centuries.

replies.title: Re: More American History
replies.content: Yes indeed! That's why Thomas Morton and Merry Mount features in the backstory of my novel The Weird of Hali: Providence. Thanks for this.

comments.title: Gods again
comments.content:
Hi JMG and all,

Last week I asked about working with Tolkein's gods, thanks for the answers.

I guess I have a more general question, how would I know which gods to engage with? To reference in the sphere of protection or to celebrate the seasonal cycle? There seem to be a lot of possibilities, and to me they are mostly equally dry and abstract ideas.

That is to say, I don't have emotional resonance or interaction with any gods except for precisely two (emotionally intense) times in my life. Although, I cannot now recall which of these incidents came first.

One time was during my druid self initiation ceremony, I became aware of a female prescence who didn't stay long and spoke precisely four words to me. She wasn't embodied, and didn't leave me with a name. She was definitely closely associated with the living, natural world.

The other time was on a flight in to Hong Kong during a typhoon (hurricane). We had circled out at sea for about 90 minutes waiting for the weather to calm down before landing. On approach we were tossed about quite violently before the captain aborted the attempt about 150 feet off the ground. I can still remember clearly the engines winding back up to full throttle and the lane climbing back up through the tempest, deadly silence from the crew.

It wasn't until we'd gained about 5000 feet and headed back out to sea that the captain announced "don't worry, we have enough fuel to go around again". I suppose that was meant to be reassuring but for me it had the opposite effect. You're probably aware that aircraft carry a contingency of extra fuel, which would have already burned most of circling over the south china sea for over an hour. What would happen if we were unable to land again? It was at that point that I was genuinely terrified for the first and only time in my life. I prayed to whoever I thought might be able to help - Jesus, Allah, Thor, and GuanYin. As we aproached the airport, the weather cleared and we landed in perfect calm.

It felt like we were helped that night, but no-one "put their hand up', so to speak. I like to imagine that GuanYin intervened with Thor on our behalf, because it sure felt like he had intended to smash us to bits.

So that's what I have: bits and pieces from very different traditions on an atheistic background. An intellectual acceptance of gods and spirits, limited and somewhat incoherent experience. No-one that I'm confident in naming.

Any advice?

Thanks,
Graeme

The important thing is to make a choice and do something, and not sit there circling in the air...  ;-)

comments.title: New Thought
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I have recently started working through the Order of Essenes lessons and see that it is referred too as a New Thought school. How does a New Thought school compare to Druidry or the Golden Dawn tradition? replies.title: Re: New Thought
replies.content: Conveniently enough, one of my recent blog posts on the magical history of America talks about the origins of New Thought. Burks Hamner's Order of Essenes comes from what I've called the pragmatic wing of New Thought, the side of the tradition that focused on stuff that actually works; the other wing, which might best be called the dogmatic wing, pushed certain New Thought theories to their illogical conclusion. The Order of Essenes focuses entirely on personal development and attaining happiness and success in life, by getting rid of unproductive habits of thought; it doesn't teach ritual magic or divination, though it does teach certain kinds of meditation. For what it's worth, I completed the Order of Essenes course while simultaneously practicing the AODA variety of Druidry, and then did it again while working with the methods of the Celtic Golden Dawn, with good results each time.

(e) I assume that only conjunctions by a benefic planet are beneficial; in the case of other non-malefic bodies, such as the Moon or Mercury, I assume that only sextiles or trines produced by them are beneficial.

comments.title: Spagyrics equipment
comments.content:
Hullo JMG!

I hope you are well,

And happy soon to be new year!

I have a couple questions about 2 of the items for the first work of spagyrics in the CGD.

Does the small iron pan need to be cast iron? I assume one of those mystery material "non stick" pans will not suffice, but will a glazed cast iron work? (Unfortunately it was given to me and I don't know what its glazed with) And for what reason does it need to be iron?

Does it matter if the pan has been used for other purposes, and/or is there any kind of Etheric cleansing you might recommend for an older used pan?

As for the iron rod, I'll probably look for something from home depot or lowes.

And as for the mortar and pestle, my girlfriend got me a ceramic one for Christmas. Is that suitable for Spagyrics, or does it specifically need to be porcelain, and if it does, and you have the time to provide an extended answer, what are the magical reasons porcelain is better suited than ceramic?

Thank you for your time and wisdom as always,

-WindMan

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics equipment
replies.content:
1) The pan needs to be plain iron. Meditate on the symbolism of iron and you should have no trouble figuring out why.

2) If it's been used for something else, wash it thoroughly, then heat it very hot to carbonize any remaining organic material, then wash it thoroughly again.

2) Ceramic is fine. Porcelain is a kind of ceramic.

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics equipment
replies.content: Not OP. If the pan comes already coated (perhaps with PTFE or whatever), will it still be suitable? Can you clarify what "plain iron" is for non-native English speakers?

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics equipment
replies.content: No, it will not be suitable. "Plain iron" means just iron, without anything on the inside surfaces but iron. If there's anything in contact with the herbs but iron, it's not plain iron.

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics equipment
replies.content:
Normally, an iron pan must be "prepared" by heating it oiled.
Is this alright?

replies.title: Re: Spagyrics equipment
replies.content: If you reread what I just wrote and pay attention to it, you'll see that I've already answered this question.

The part of the FAQ about "more complex than" might seem straightforward to you, but you already know, not only which distinction it is supposed to refer to, but that it is even meant to be read informally as a reference to an (elsewhere-defined) distinction in the first place. Until I found the mention of this question in the MM thread of Jul 25 2018, my only concrete guess was that "more complex than" was meant to be read literally, as a reference to a threshold on a continuum of complexity, immediately above the level of SoP/LRP/MP, so that there was little or no margin for error.

I still don't know how I would tell for sure whether divine energies might get accidentally brought down etc., or even whether I really should breathe easy and assume that the omission of an explicit explanation of what "more complex than" means is unintentional and doesn't mean anything about the margin for error, but I suppose these things can wait.

comments.title: Getting Beyond the Narratives
comments.content:
In the essay “Getting Beyond the Narratives” you write... “the structures of consciousness are tools of magic. In the system of magic I practice, those structures are identified with the numbers from one to ten, understood not as quantities but as abstract relationships. You can experience anything through any number (though numbers above ten denote relationships too complex for the human nervous system to handle). Each number has its strengths and its weaknesses. If you’re working deliberately with the structures of consciousness – which is to say, if you’re a mage – you choose the structure number you use based on the effects you want to get. Which system of magic did you have in mind when you wrote that paragraph?

Where could I learn more about the structures of consciousness identified with numbers one to ten? Have you discussed this in a book? Perhaps could you point to essays on this website?

May you have a refreshing time during January!

May everything go well, for all of us, in this coming year!!

EllenZ
